Checkpoint 5: Final report — root cause and every location to change

Root cause
- Seaborn code (PairGrid and related faceting logic) indexes DataFrame columns with keys coming from variable names. When the DataFrame has MultiIndex columns (column labels are tuples), bracket-access like data[tuple_label] can be misinterpreted by pandas as an iterable (list of labels) rather than a single label, in some pandas versions/contexts, causing KeyError: "['1'] not in index".
- The user-observed failure chain is:
  - pairplot -> PairGrid -> map_diag -> vector = self.data[var] (var is tuple) -> pandas interprets tuple as iterable -> __getitem__ expands to list(['A','1']) -> MultiIndex._get_indexer_strict cannot find '1' at top-level -> KeyError.

All locations that need change (minimum set to fix pairplot reliably)
- seaborn/utils.py
  - Add new helper:
    - def index_variable(data, key): try data[key] except (KeyError, TypeError): return data.loc[:, key]
  - Add to __all__.

- seaborn/axisgrid.py (multiple places)
  - Import the helper:
    - from .utils import index_variable
  - Replace direct bracket indexing with safe indexing:
    - Grid._get_palette: categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order) -> categorical_order(index_variable(data, hue), hue_order)
    - FacetGrid.facet_data: data[self._row_var] etc -> index_variable(data, self._row_var)
    - PairGrid._find_numeric_cols: variable_type(data[col]) -> variable_type(index_variable(data, col))
    - PairGrid.map_diag: vector = self.data[var] -> vector = index_variable(self.data, var)
    - PairGrid.map_diag: hue = self.data[self._hue_var] -> hue = index_variable(self.data, self._hue_var)
    - PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue: hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(...) -> hue_grouped = index_variable(self.data, var).groupby(...)
    - PairGrid._plot_bivariate: data = self.data[axes_vars] -> data = self.data.loc[:, axes_vars]; x/y replaced with data.loc[:, x_var], data.loc[:, y_var]
    - PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue: x = data_k[x_var] -> x = data_k.loc[:, x_var]; y similarly.

- seaborn/_oldcore.py (VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform)
  - Import index_variable from .utils.
  - Replace plot_data[key] = data[val] with plot_data[key] = index_variable(data, val) in the branch that checks 'if val in data'.

Rationale and reasoning for every change
- Why utils.index_variable:
  - Centralizes the robust access pattern for DataFrame column selection where column labels may be tuples.
- Why axisgrid.py changes:
  - PairGrid is the class that triggers the KeyError in pairplot. All code paths in PairGrid that index the raw DataFrame with variable labels may encounter the same misinterpretation of tuple keys; using index_variable (or .loc for list-of-columns) prevents false expansion of tuple into iterable elements.
- Why _oldcore change:
  - VectorPlotter.assign_variables_longform is responsible for converting user-supplied long-form data references into Series. PairGrid/Pairplot workflows call this logic in some contexts; making it robust prevents upstream failures.

Tests and expected behavior after fix
- The MRE in the bug report should work:

    import numpy as np
    import pandas as pd
    import seaborn as sns

    data = {
        ("A", "1"): np.random.rand(100),
        ("A", "2"): np.random.rand(100),
        ("B", "1"): np.random.rand(100),
        ("B", "2"): np.random.rand(100),
    }
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)
    sns.pairplot(df)  # should not raise KeyError; plots drawn as expected

- Existing functionality for non-MultiIndex columns is preserved:
  - index_variable will try data[key] and succeed; the fallback won't be exercised.

Notes and further considerations
- The underlying root problem is a mismatch between how Seaborn uses DataFrame bracket indexing and differences in pandas behavior about whether an iterable key (tuple) is treated as a single label or a sequence of labels. The patch makes Seaborn robust to that discrepancy.
- A more comprehensive follow-up could:
  - Replace other direct data[some_var] usages across seaborn modules with index_variable (I identified other places that call categorical_order(data[var]) and similar patterns). I limited changes here to the PairGrid / VectorPlotter code paths that directly led to the user-reported KeyError for pairplot.
  - Add unit tests that exercise pairplot with MultiIndex columns and other seaborn functions that accept DataFrames with MultiIndex columns.
- I intentionally kept the change minimal and non-invasive: try the fast path (data[key]) first for performance, then fallback to .loc for the edge case. This preserves behavior and performance in the common case while fixing the bug.

If you want, I can:
- Produce exact patch/diff snippets for each file (utils.py, axisgrid.py, _oldcore.py) showing the exact lines to change.
- Run a search for other occurrences of data[var] / categorical_order(data[var]) across the repo and propose a more complete patch set to make the library universally robust to MultiIndex column labels.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: pairplot (PairGrid) indexes DataFrame columns with variable-derived keys using bracket-access (e.g., self.data[var]). When columns are a pandas.MultiIndex (column labels are tuples), pandas can interpret a tuple key as an iterable of labels and expand it, producing a KeyError like "['1'] not in index". The wrong assumption is that DataFrame.__getitem__(key) will always treat a tuple as a single column label.
- Root cause (where it lives): seaborn/axisgrid.py — PairGrid.map_diag and other PairGrid/FacetGrid/ Grid indexing sites call data[var] (and related patterns); the traceback shows the failure flows from axisgrid.py -> pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ -> pandas.core.indexes.multi._get_indexer_strict. (See stack trace and source reads in Checkpoints 1–3.)
- Fix strategy: add a small, centralized safe accessor (utils.index_variable) that tries data[key] (fast path) and falls back to label-based selection data.loc[:, key] when KeyError/TypeError occurs; then replace direct bracket indexing in the PairGrid/faceting code path (and VectorPlotter long-form variable extraction) to use this helper or explicit .loc for multi-column selection. This covers the pairplot path and prevents the observed KeyError.
- Hardcoded values: no numeric constants need changing — the erroneous assumption is encoded in the code pattern data[key] itself. The change is behavioral: use index_variable(...) or .loc rather than relying on bracket semantics.
- New/changed methods: add utils.index_variable(data, key). Replace several lines in seaborn/axisgrid.py and one in seaborn/_oldcore.py (VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform). No class overrides are necessary; we only change how columns are read.
- Investigation gaps: I did not exhaustively replace every other data[var] occurrence across the repo (search shows other places that call categorical_order(data[var]) such as seaborn/_core/groupby.py, seaborn/_core/plot.py, seaborn/_core/properties.py). Those are likely to require the same defensive access but are outside the immediate pairplot call path — they should be updated in a follow-up pass and unit-tested.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: PairGrid.map_diag indexes DataFrame with tuple keys (root cause)
What: PairGrid.map_diag contains "vector = self.data[var]" where var can be a tuple (MultiIndex column label). This led directly to the reported KeyError.  
Where: seaborn/axisgrid.py, class PairGrid, method map_diag.  
Why it matters: If pandas interprets tuple as iterable, __getitem__ expands it to list(tuple) and pandas throws KeyError (observed: "['1'] not in index"). (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 2)

### Finding 2: Pandas __getitem__ + MultiIndex tuple key misinterpretation
What: The stack trace shows pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ treats the key as an iterator and calls self.columns._get_indexer_strict(key,...), which then calls _get_indexer_level_0 and raises KeyError "['1'] not in index".  
Where (evidence): stack trace from bug report: axisgrid.py -> map_diag -> vector = self.data[var] -> pandas/core/frame.py __getitem__ -> multi.py _get_indexer_strict -> KeyError.  
Why it matters: The failure is in pandas selection semantics; seaborn must defensively access columns to avoid this. (Found in Checkpoint 1a)

### Finding 3: Multiple PairGrid / Grid / FacetGrid sites use direct indexing and must be fixed
What: The same unsafe pattern appears in PairGrid._find_numeric_cols (variable_type(data[col])), PairGrid._plot_bivariate (data = self.data[axes_vars]; x = data[x_var]), PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue, Grid._get_palette (categorical_order(data[hue])), and FacetGrid.facet_data (data[self._row_var]/self._col_var/self._hue_var).  
Files/methods: seaborn/axisgrid.py — PairGrid._find_numeric_cols, PairGrid._plot_bivariate, PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue, PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue, Grid._get_palette, FacetGrid.facet_data.  
Why it matters: All these are on the pairplot/faceting call path and can raise the same KeyError for MultiIndex columns. They must be changed consistently. (Found in Checkpoint 2 / 3)

### Finding 4: VectorPlotter.long-form variable extraction uses data[val] and needs same defense
What: VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform checks "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]". If val is a tuple (MultiIndex column label), data[val] may raise KeyError for same reason.  
File/method: seaborn/_oldcore.py, class VectorPlotter, method _assign_variables_longform.  
Why it matters: PairGrid and other high-level APIs use VectorPlotter to canonicalize variables; making VectorPlotter robust prevents upstream failures. (Found in Checkpoint 3)

### Finding 5: Concrete low-risk fix — add utils.index_variable and change reads
What: Add a helper utils.index_variable(data, key) that tries data[key] and falls back to data.loc[:, key] on KeyError/TypeError; then use it in the PairGrid/FacetGrid/Grid reads and in VectorPlotter long-form extraction; for list-of-columns selection use explicit data.loc[:, axes_vars].  
Why it matters: Minimal, localized, preserves fast-path and fixes tuple-as-iterable failure. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 6: Investigation omissions / next steps
What: I did not update every place that calls categorical_order(data[var]) or other direct bracket-access across the repo (search hits include seaborn/_core/groupby.py:57, seaborn/_core/plot.py:1100/1581, seaborn/_core/properties.py multiple lines). These are candidate sites that may fail for MultiIndex labels in other workflows.  
Why it matters: To be complete, the same defensive accessor should be applied to other modules; also add unit tests exercising MultiIndex columns across seaborn APIs. (Gap identified in Checkpoint 3)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:PairGrid.map_diag
- Wrong assumption: The code assumes data[var] treats tuple var as a single column label.
- Evidence: map_diag contains "vector = self.data[var]" (axisgrid.py line ~1490); the user stack trace shows this call leads to pandas __getitem__ expanding a tuple and raising KeyError "['1'] not in index". (Stack trace excerpt in bug report; found in Checkpoint 1c)
- Related locations: PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue (uses self.data[var].groupby), PairGrid._plot_bivariate (uses data[x_var], data[y_var).

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:PairGrid._find_numeric_cols
- Wrong assumption: variable_type(data[col]) is safe for arbitrary column labels.
- Evidence: _find_numeric_cols iterates "for col in data:" then calls variable_type(data[col]) (axisgrid.py ~1664). If col is a tuple, data[col] may be misinterpreted. (Found during file read in Checkpoint 2)
- Related locations: PairGrid.__init__ uses _find_numeric_cols to set x_vars/y_vars.

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:PairGrid._plot_bivariate and _plot_bivariate_iter_hue
- Wrong assumption: data = self.data[axes_vars] and data[x_var] are safe when x_var/y_var are tuple labels.
- Evidence: code at axisgrid.py ~1590–1610 sets data = self.data[axes_vars]; then later x = data[x_var] / y = data[y_var]. These forms triggered the same kind of pandas expansion bug in experiments; using .loc[:, axes_vars] and .loc[:, x_var] avoids it. (Identified in Checkpoint 2 / 3)
- Related locations: PairGrid.map_diag and PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue.

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:FacetGrid.facet_data
- Wrong assumption: data[self._row_var]/data[self._col_var]/data[self._hue_var] are always safe single-key bracket accesses.
- Evidence: facet_data builds row_masks/col_masks/hue_masks using direct bracket-access (axisgrid.py ~637–688). If those vars are tuple labels (e.g., user passed a MultiIndex column as facet variable), bracket-access can raise the same KeyError. (Read in Checkpoint 2)
- Related locations: Grid._get_palette also indexes hue similarly.

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:Grid._get_palette
- Wrong assumption: categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order) is safe for arbitrary hue keys.
- Evidence: Grid._get_palette calls categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order) (axisgrid.py ~244) — same bracket-access pattern; if hue is a tuple label, this can fail. (Observed in file read in Checkpoint 2)
- Related locations: All Grid-derived classes using hue selection (PairGrid, FacetGrid).

### Location: seaborn/_oldcore.py:VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform
- Wrong assumption: in long-form variable assignment "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]" bracket-access is safe for all val.
- Evidence: code in _oldcore.py around the long-form assignment uses data[val] (see lines ~920–936); if val is tuple label, data[val] can raise KeyError the same as in PairGrid. (Read in Checkpoint 3)
- Related locations: VectorPlotter._assign_variables_wideform; any code that relies on VectorPlotter to canonicalize variables for plotting (i.e., many seaborn high-level APIs).

### Location: (new) seaborn/utils.py:index_variable (helper to add)
- Wrong assumption being fixed: that bracket-access alone suffices for MultiIndex tuple labels.
- Evidence: Add helper:

    def index_variable(data, key):
        """Safely index a DataFrame by a column key that may be a tuple (MultiIndex)."""
        try:
            return data[key]
        except (KeyError, TypeError):
            return data.loc[:, key]

  This preserves fast path and falls back to label-based selection; use this where direct bracket indexing of variable-derived keys is performed. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4)

### Candidate locations not yet patched (investigation gap)
- Wrong assumption: many places assume data[var] is always safe.
- Evidence: search results show categorical_order(data[var]) or similar patterns in:
  - seaborn/_core/groupby.py: line 57
  - seaborn/_core/plot.py: lines 1100 and 1581
  - seaborn/_core/properties.py: lines 225, 363, 675, 768
  - and others (search results surfaced these; full pass required). (Identified in Checkpoint 3)
- Related locations: The full suite of seaborn modules that accept DataFrame column names as arguments — these should be audited and switched to index_variable or .loc where appropriate.

--- End of report.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify each of the diagnostic claims in the draft by inspecting the seaborn source cited and the investigation findings. For each tool request below I state why I need the file and what I expect to learn; after each request I summarize what I found.

1) Verify PairGrid.map_diag / PairGrid.map_diag-related claims
- Why I fetched axisgrid.py: to locate the PairGrid methods (map_diag, _find_numeric_cols, _plot_bivariate, _map_diag_iter_hue, _plot_bivariate_iter_hue) and check whether they index DataFrame columns with direct bracket-access (data[var], data[col], self.data[var], etc.), which would be subject to the reported pandas MultiIndex tuple-as-iterable behavior.
- What I expected: find direct bracket-access usages of variable-derived keys.

What I retrieved (selected, with line numbers shown in file):
- PairGrid.map_diag:
  - Line ~1490: "vector = self.data[var]"
  - Line ~1491: "if self._hue_var is not None: hue = self.data[self._hue_var]"
  This confirms the draft's assertion that map_diag indexes the DataFrame with self.data[var].

- PairGrid._find_numeric_cols:
  - Lines 1664–1670:
    - "for col in data:"
    - "    if variable_type(data[col]) == 'numeric':"
  This confirms the code iterates column names and does variable_type(data[col]) — i.e., data[col] is used.

- PairGrid._plot_bivariate:
  - Lines 1591–1599:
    - "data = self.data[axes_vars]"
    - "x = data[x_var]"
    - "y = data[y_var]"
  This confirms the draft's claim that axes are selected via self.data[axes_vars], and then columns within that subset are accessed with data[x_var] etc.

- PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue / _map_diag_iter_hue:
  - Line ~1519: "hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(self.hue_vals)"
  - Lines ~1529–1533: uses hue_grouped.get_group(label_k)
  These also show bracket-access self.data[var] and groupby on the resulting Series.

- PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue:
  - Lines ~1639–1642:
    - "if self._dropna: data_k = data_k[axes_vars].dropna()"
    - "x = data_k[x_var]; y = data_k[y_var]"
  Again confirms bracket-access on variable-derived keys.

Conclusion: The draft's claims about the PairGrid methods using direct bracket-access (self.data[var], data[col], data[axes_vars]) are correct and supported by the code.

2) Verify Grid._get_palette and FacetGrid.facet_data claims
- Why I fetched axisgrid.py lines earlier: same file contains Grid and FacetGrid code. I expected to find categorical_order(data[hue]) and data[self._row_var] usage.

What I retrieved:
- Grid._get_palette:
  - Line ~250: "hue_names = categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order)"
  This shows bracket-access to data[hue] and passing that to categorical_order.

- FacetGrid.__init__ and facet_data:
  - Lines ~383/391/396: several categorical_order(data[hue]/data[row]/data[col]) in __init__ — e.g., "hue_names = categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order)" and "row_names = categorical_order(data[row], row_order)" and "col_names = categorical_order(data[col], col_order)".
  - facet_data (lines ~652–667):
    - "row_masks = [data[self._row_var] == n for n in self.row_names]"
    - "col_masks = [data[self._col_var] == n for n in self.col_names]"
    - "hue_masks = [data[self._hue_var] == n for n in self.hue_names]"
  These show direct bracket-access for the faceting variables.

Conclusion: The draft's claim that Grid._get_palette and FacetGrid.facet_data use bracket-indexing on variable-derived keys is correct.

3) Verify VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform claims
- Why I fetched _oldcore.py: to inspect VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform to confirm whether it uses "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]" and similar direct bracket-access.
- What I expected: to find the exact code the draft references.

What I retrieved:
- In _assign_variables_longform (lines ~920–936 and around 930–936):
  - The code checks membership with:
    try:
        val_as_data_key = (val in data or (isinstance(val, (str, bytes)) and val in index))
    except (KeyError, TypeError):
        val_as_data_key = False
  - If val_as_data_key:
    - "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]"
    - "elif val in index: plot_data[key] = index[val]"
  This exactly matches the pattern described in the draft.

Conclusion: The draft's claim about VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform using bracket-access (data[val]) is accurate.

4) Verify the pandas behavior claim and stack trace evidence
- The original bug report includes a stack trace showing seaborn/axisgrid.py map_diag calling "vector = self.data[var]" which triggers pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ and then pandas.core.indexes.multi._get_indexer_strict raising KeyError "['1'] not in index".
- I did not fetch pandas internals here (out of scope), but the user-provided traceback is concrete evidence that passing a tuple-derived key into DataFrame.__getitem__ in this case led to pandas interpreting the tuple as an iterable of column labels and raising a KeyError that names the expanded elements (e.g., "['1'] not in index"). The seaborn code lines I inspected align with the traceback (axisgrid.py:1490 vector = self.data[var]).

Conclusion: The draft's behavioral description of the pandas selection semantics (tuple being interpreted as an iterable and expanded to multiple labels, causing KeyError) is consistent with the included traceback and with where seaborn calls are made.

5) Check for claims in the draft that are not supported by code evidence
- The draft includes specific proposed fixes (e.g., "add utils.index_variable", "change code to use .loc or helper"). Those are implementation proposals (a fix strategy) present in the Investigation Analysis, and they appear in the draft's RootCause (items that describe a helper). Per the reviewer guidelines, a bug diagnosis must not prescribe fixes. These fix-proposal statements are not "claims" about the cause but are prescriptive and thus must be removed from the RootCause field. The code evidence does not support a single "must be changed this exact way" claim — it only supports that the pattern self.data[var] is unsafe for MultiIndex tuple labels.

- The draft's RootCause item 7 (Scope gap) says "Search hits: seaborn/_core/groupby.py, seaborn/_core/plot.py, seaborn/_core/properties.py" — I verified those hits exist in the repo search and inspected some of them. For example:
  - seaborn/_core/groupby.py (line ~57) calls categorical_order(data[var]) after checking var in data.
  - seaborn/_core/plot.py (lines ~1100 and ~1581) call categorical_order(common.frame[dim]) / categorical_order(df[var]).
  - seaborn/_core/properties.py (line ~225/363/675/768) does categorical_order(data, order) in various property code paths.
  These are valid findings; they are not prescriptive and should be included in RootCause as additional locations sharing the same risky pattern. The draft's scope-gap phrasing "these locations were identified but not exhaustively audited" is appropriate as a note, but in the RootCause we must elevate these findings to be equal-weight items (per the guidelines: include all secondary findings from the investigation analysis that share the same root cause pattern).

6) Impact completeness check — are all affected code paths and dependent code identified?
- From inspection:
  - The primary problematic pattern is "indexing a pandas DataFrame with a variable-derived key via __getitem__ using bracket-access (data[key]) where key may be a tuple (MultiIndex column label)". I verified this pattern in multiple places:
    - axisgrid.py: PairGrid.map_diag (vector = self.data[var]), PairGrid._find_numeric_cols (data[col]), PairGrid._plot_bivariate (data = self.data[axes_vars]; x = data[x_var]), PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue, PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue.
    - axisgrid.py (Grid / FacetGrid): Grid._get_palette (categorical_order(data[hue])), FacetGrid.__init__/facet_data (data[row], data[col], data[hue]).
    - _oldcore.py: VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform (if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]).
  - The search for categorical_order(...) revealed many other uses in seaborn modules (e.g., _core/groupby.py, _core/plot.py, _core/properties.py, categorical.py, and others). These sites pass data[var] into categorical_order or otherwise compute orders from a Series derived via bracket-access. If var could be a tuple label, those calls are at risk.
  - The draft's Investigation Analysis flagged more candidates (e.g., seaborn/_core/groupby.py, seaborn/_core/plot.py, seaborn/_core/properties.py). I verified these hits exist.

Conclusion: The draft identified the main problematic patterns and key locations on the pairplot/faceting code path. There are multiple other locations across seaborn that use the same pattern; the draft listed a subset of them. Per the requirement to include all secondary findings from the investigation analysis, I will ensure the revised RootCause gives equal emphasis to the additional files identified in the investigation analysis.

7) Field discipline check: ensure fields contain only appropriate content
- Description: The draft description is largely appropriate (concise summary of what goes wrong when passing MultiIndex tuple labels into pairplot) — acceptable but I will tighten wording to avoid any implementation hint or fix suggestions.
- RootCause: The draft mixes diagnosis and fix proposals (helper). Per the guidelines, RootCause must only describe incorrect assumptions and the locations that encode them; I will remove any fix proposals and reformat RootCause as a set of equally-weighted findings listing each incorrect assumption, the exact location(s), and a short quote of the code line(s) as evidence. For multi-location findings that are essentially the same pattern, each location must be enumerated and given equal weight.
- ExpectedBehavior and ObservedBehavior: both are correctly focused on user-visible behavior; I will keep them but may tighten wording.

8) Unsupported or overstated claims
- The diagnosis that "DataFrame.__getitem__ will always treat a tuple key as a single column label" is indeed the incorrect assumption in seaborn code; the evidence supports that pandas will sometimes interpret a tuple as an iterable for selecting multiple columns (as shown by the user's traceback). The draft does not overstate pandas behavior beyond the stack trace; so keep the description but cite stack-trace evidence.
- The draft's specific fix suggestion must be removed from the RootCause and moved to 'changes' as "removed fix suggestion" (the report must contain only diagnosis). I will not include any fix instructions in the revised report.

9) Missing important findings from investigation analysis
- The draft's RootCause enumerated many PairGrid/Fgrid locations and included a "scope gap" note. The Investigation Analysis explicitly listed groupby.py, plot.py, properties.py occurrences. I verified those exist. The revised RootCause must explicitly list these additional files as locations that share the same root cause and give them equal weight.

  - PairGrid.map_diag: "vector = self.data[var]" (axisgrid.py ~1490)
  - PairGrid._find_numeric_cols: "for col in data: if variable_type(data[col]) == 'numeric'" (axisgrid.py ~1664-1669)

Phase 1 conclusion and required edits to the draft
- The core technical claims in the draft are supported by the code: seaborn often uses direct bracket-access (data[var] / self.data[var] / data[axes_vars]) with variable-derived keys; if those keys are tuple labels from a pandas.MultiIndex, pandas may interpret the tuple as an iterable and attempt multi-column selection, which can raise KeyError as shown in the traceback.
- The draft contains proposed fixes (helper utils/index_variable and explicit code changes). Those are not part of a diagnosis and must be removed from the RootCause.
- The draft's RootCause must be rewritten to present each incorrect assumption and the specific locations where it occurs (including the extra files flagged in the investigation analysis) with equal weight, and with short quoted evidence lines from the code.
- The StepsToReproduce, ExpectedBehavior, and ObservedBehavior are appropriate; I will make minor wording adjustments for clarity.

bug_report.Title: pairplot raises KeyError with MultiIndex DataFrame
bug_report.Description: Calling sns.pairplot (PairGrid) on a pandas DataFrame whose columns are a MultiIndex (tuple labels) can raise a KeyError. In several places the seaborn code indexes dataframe columns with direct bracket-access using variable-derived keys (e.g. self.data[var] or data[col]); when such a key is a tuple, pandas may interpret it as an iterable and attempt multi-column selection, triggering a KeyError (for example: "['1'] not in index").
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: Indexing a DataFrame with a variable-derived key using bracket-access will always treat a tuple as a single column label.
   Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py, PairGrid.map_diag
   Evidence (code): "vector = self.data[var]" (axisgrid.py ~1490).

2) Incorrect assumption: Iterating DataFrame columns and accessing each column via data[col] is safe for all column label types.
   Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py, PairGrid._find_numeric_cols
   Evidence (code): "for col in data: if variable_type(data[col]) == 'numeric'" (axisgrid.py ~1664-1669).

3) Incorrect assumption: Subsetting data with data[axes_vars] and then indexing that result with data[x_var]/data[y_var] is safe when axes_vars/x_var/y_var may be tuple labels.
   Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py, PairGrid._plot_bivariate and PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue
   Evidence (code): "data = self.data[axes_vars]" and "x = data[x_var]" / "y = data[y_var]" (axisgrid.py ~1594, 1598-1599; ~1639-1642).

4) Incorrect assumption: Faceting and palette selection logic can index faceting/hue variables with bracket-access safely for any label types.
   Locations: seaborn/axisgrid.py, FacetGrid.facet_data and Grid._get_palette
   Evidence (code): "row_masks = [data[self._row_var] == n ...]" / "col_masks = [data[self._col_var] == n ...]" / "hue_masks = [data[self._hue_var] == n ...]" (axisgrid.py ~652-667); "hue_names = categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order)" (axisgrid.py ~250).

5) Incorrect assumption: Long-form variable canonicalization can test membership and then index by val via bracket-access safely for all label types.
   Location: seaborn/_oldcore.py, VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform
   Evidence (code): membership test then "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]" ( _oldcore.py ~921-935).

6) Behavioral cause (pandas selection semantics): Pandas DataFrame.__getitem__ may treat a tuple key as an iterable of column labels (expanding it) and delegate to index selection logic that can raise a KeyError naming the expanded components (user traceback shows pandas.core.indexes.multi._get_indexer_strict raising "['1'] not in index"). This is the mechanism by which tuple-labeled columns lead to the observed exceptions when seaborn passes tuple labels to bracket-access.

7) Additional occurrences across the codebase that share the same risky pattern: several modules call categorical_order(data[var]) or otherwise call categorical_order on a Series obtained by bracket-access; those call sites can similarly be affected if var is a tuple MultiIndex label.
   Examples: seaborn/_core/groupby.py (categorical_order(data[var]) — group construction) ; seaborn/_core/plot.py (categorical_order(common.frame[dim] / df[var]) in subplot/facet setup); seaborn/_core/properties.py (categorical_order(data, order) in scale/property code paths). These locations were identified by code search and must be considered part of the same root-cause pattern.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following minimal example in a Python session with pandas and seaborn installed:
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: pairplot should complete and draw the pairwise plots for the DataFrame. Column labels that are tuple-valued (pandas.MultiIndex column labels) should be treated as single labels when passed as variable keys to seaborn plotting functions (no KeyError raised).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Calling sns.pairplot on a DataFrame with MultiIndex column labels raises a KeyError (e.g. "['1'] not in index"). The traceback shows seaborn calling e.g. PairGrid.map_diag -> vector = self.data[var], which propagates into pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ and then into pandas.core.indexes.multi._get_indexer_strict where the KeyError originates.
